title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Poirot reflects on his conversation with Mrs. Samoys of the Long Grass Hotel, noting that the victim, Mrs. McGinty, used to assist her. A profile of Mrs. McGinty emerges: she was a solitary woman who knitted, polished floors, and shined brass. A Conservative voter who disapproved of immoral behavior, she enjoyed magazines and movie star gossip but did not read books or watch many films.

Poirot observes Joe Burch arriving home for lunch. Unlike his relaxed wife, Joe appears tense but eager to cooperate, likely because Poirot possesses a letter of introduction from Inspector Spence. Poirot contemplates whether Joe has a guilty conscience and considers the possibility that Joe attacked Aunt Ada to steal money for his wife, potentially framing James Bentley. Poirot also notes that the murder weapon—described by forensics as an unusual, sharp object similar to a meat cleaver—has not been found, leading him to wonder if it is hidden within the Burch household.

Later, Poirot visits the Bredice & Scott office to interview Mr. Scott regarding James Bentley. To gain Scott's cooperation, Poirot lies, claiming he is investigating on behalf of Bentley's wealthy, powerful relatives. Mr. Scott, a talkative man, describes Bentley as a decent but ineffective junior staff member who lacked sales skills. When asked if he believes Bentley committed the murder, Scott suggests that while Bentley lacks the "nerve" for such an act, the man's mental state was unstable due to unemployment and stress, making it psychologically possible.
